courses.title: Moisture, Mould, and Indoor Air Quality: Understanding the Link
courses.description:
Welcome to Moisture, Mould, and Indoor Air Quality: Understanding the Link — a comprehensive course designed for professionals in the restoration, building, environmental health, and facilities management industries who are committed to creating healthier indoor environments.

Mould is more than just an unsightly nuisance — it’s a biological contaminant that thrives in moist environments and can have serious consequences for both building materials and occupant health. Understanding the science behind moisture intrusion, mould growth, and their impact on indoor air quality is critical to preventing costly damage and protecting public health.

This course brings together the essential knowledge and tools needed to identify, investigate, and manage moisture and mould in buildings. Whether you’re responding to water damage, improving air quality, or ensuring compliance with industry standards, this training will strengthen your foundation and improve your on-the-job decision-making.

Throughout the course, you’ll explore topics such as:

The biological behaviour of mould spores and their activation by moisture

Common sources of hidden and visible moisture in buildings

How mould impacts air quality, including chemical and biological emissions

High-risk building materials and HVAC system vulnerabilities

Health effects of exposure to mould-contaminated air

Inspection protocols, air sampling, and moisture detection tools

Drying strategies to prevent regrowth after water damage

Safe remediation practices and techniques for air quality recovery

Clear communication and professional documentation practices

This is more than a technical training—it’s a guide to better outcomes for clients, healthier indoor spaces, and professional excellence in the field of restoration and environmental health.

Let’s get started.

lessons.title: The Moisture-Mould Connection — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
The Nature of Mould Spores and Activation

Mould spores are microscopic reproductive units found naturally in the environment—both indoors and outdoors. They are lightweight, resilient, and can easily travel through the air, settling on surfaces throughout buildings. These spores are largely harmless when dry and inactive, existing in a dormant state until the environment becomes hospitable. Moisture is the critical factor that activates these spores, enabling them to germinate and grow.

Once moisture is introduced—whether through leaks, condensation, or high humidity—spores absorb water, triggering cellular processes that allow them to develop into mature mould colonies. This growth can occur rapidly, often within 24 to 48 hours, especially in warm, nutrient-rich conditions.

Sources and Types of Moisture That Encourage Growth

Moisture can infiltrate indoor environments through many routes. Common sources include plumbing leaks, roof leaks, floodwater intrusion, condensation on cold surfaces, and elevated indoor humidity caused by insufficient ventilation or occupant activities such as cooking, bathing, and drying clothes indoors.

Not all moisture is visible to the naked eye. For example, capillary action can draw groundwater up into foundation walls, a process known as rising damp. Condensation can accumulate inside wall cavities, behind insulation, or in ductwork, creating hidden reservoirs of moisture where mould can thrive unnoticed for long periods.

The Impact of Moisture on Mould Growth and Air Quality

Active mould colonies do more than just affect surfaces—they become a persistent source of airborne contamination. As mould grows, it sheds spores and fragments into the air. These particles are microscopic and easily inhaled, posing health risks to building occupants. Additionally, mould releases microbial volatile organic compounds (MVOCs), which contribute to musty odours and chemical irritation.

Because spores can travel through air currents and HVAC systems, mould contamination is rarely limited to the visibly affected areas. The presence of moisture thus indirectly compromises the entire building’s indoor air quality. This is why controlling moisture is the cornerstone of mould prevention and remediation strategies.

lessons.title: Identifying Common Moisture Sources — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Visible Signs of Moisture Intrusion

Water stains on ceilings, walls, or floors are classic indicators of moisture problems. Bubbling or peeling paint, warped or sagging drywall, and visible mould growth all suggest ongoing moisture issues. Leaks around plumbing fixtures, basements with wet floors, and areas near sump pumps or drainage systems are typical hotspots.

Regular inspections for these signs can help catch moisture problems early, preventing mould from gaining a foothold. However, relying solely on visual signs is insufficient, as many moisture issues remain hidden.

Hidden and Subtle Moisture Problems

Many moisture issues are concealed within building assemblies. Water can enter through small cracks in foundations, behind siding, or through poorly sealed window frames. Inside walls, damp insulation or wall cavities can harbor moisture for months or years, feeding hidden mould growth.

HVAC systems are also a common culprit. Condensation can form on cold surfaces like evaporator coils or inside drain pans, causing microbial growth that can disperse spores throughout the building’s ventilation system. Poor ventilation exacerbates these issues by trapping moisture inside the building envelope.

Tools and Technology for Detecting Moisture

Moisture detection requires a combination of specialized equipment and expertise. Moisture meters, including pin and pinless types, measure moisture content within materials, providing immediate data to assess water presence and severity. Thermal imaging cameras detect temperature differences that often correlate with moisture intrusion.

Hygrometers measure the relative humidity (RH) of indoor air. Sustained RH above 60% is highly conducive to mould growth, so monitoring these levels is essential in both detecting existing problems and preventing future issues. When combined with thorough visual inspection, these tools provide a robust strategy for identifying moisture sources before mould spreads.

modules.title: How Mould Affects Indoor Air Quality

lessons.title: How Mould Affects Indoor Air Quality — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Airborne Biological Contaminants From Mould Growth

Mould produces a variety of biological contaminants that impact air quality. These include spores, tiny reproductive structures that become airborne and inhaled, and hyphal fragments—small pieces of mould colonies that break off and become airborne. Both can cause allergic reactions and respiratory irritation.

Some mould species produce mycotoxins—secondary metabolites toxic to humans and animals. Mycotoxins can exacerbate health issues and pose particular risks to vulnerable populations such as children, the elderly, and those with compromised immune systems. While not all moulds produce mycotoxins, their presence adds a layer of complexity and hazard to indoor air quality.

Chemical Byproducts: Microbial Volatile Organic Compounds (MVOCs)

Mould metabolism emits MVOCs, which are responsible for the musty, earthy odours often associated with damp buildings. MVOCs are volatile chemicals that can cause irritation of the eyes, nose, and throat. Prolonged exposure to these compounds may worsen symptoms in sensitive individuals and reduce overall comfort in indoor spaces.

The presence of persistent musty odours is often an important clue indicating hidden mould growth or ongoing moisture problems—even if mould is not visible. Addressing MVOCs usually requires eliminating the source of mould growth and thorough cleaning, not just masking the odours.

Air Quality Impact and Spread Within Buildings

Mould spores and MVOCs do not stay localized; they travel through air currents and ventilation systems, spreading contamination. In poorly maintained HVAC systems, spores can be distributed throughout multiple rooms, exacerbating exposure and making remediation more difficult.

Understanding how mould affects indoor air quality highlights why simply cleaning visible mould is insufficient. Comprehensive remediation includes moisture control, air filtration, and HVAC system assessment to prevent the ongoing spread of contaminants.

lessons.title: Building Materials and Mould Susceptibility — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
High-Risk Materials

Certain materials in buildings are particularly prone to mould growth. These include:

Drywall: Absorbs moisture quickly and provides cellulose for mould to feed on.
Carpet and padding: Trap moisture and organic particles, creating ideal mould habitats.
Insulation: Especially fiberglass and cellulose, which can trap water and support microbial growth.
Timber and particle board: Wood-based products absorb and retain moisture, often leading to decay and structural damage.
Once these materials are saturated and colonized, remediation often requires removal rather than cleaning. Their porous nature means mould grows into the material, not just on the surface.

Resistant Surfaces

Non-porous materials like glass, metal, and certain plastics are less supportive of mould, but they aren’t immune. Dust, dirt, and organic residues on these surfaces can still provide a foothold for microbial growth. Regular cleaning and controlling humidity are essential to keep even these materials mould-free.

In construction and renovation, using moisture-resistant materials in high-risk areas (e.g., cement board in bathrooms) can greatly reduce the potential for future mould issues.

modules.title: The Role of Humidity and HVAC Systems

lessons.title: The Role of Humidity and HVAC Systems — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Understanding Relative Humidity and Its Impact on Mould Growth

Relative humidity (RH) measures the amount of moisture in the air relative to the maximum it can hold at a given temperature. It plays a critical role in determining whether mould can thrive indoors. When indoor RH consistently exceeds 60%, conditions become highly favourable for mould colonization. At these levels, moisture readily condenses on cooler surfaces, saturating porous materials such as drywall, wood, and carpet—creating ideal environments for fungal growth.

Maintaining indoor RH between 30% and 50% is widely recommended to limit mould proliferation and maintain occupant comfort. However, achieving and sustaining this balance can be challenging in climates with high outdoor humidity or in buildings with poor ventilation or water intrusion issues.

Everyday Activities and Their Influence on Indoor Humidity

Everyday activities within a building can significantly increase indoor humidity levels, often unnoticed. Cooking, showering, boiling water, drying clothes indoors, and even breathing all release water vapour into the air. In tightly sealed, energy-efficient buildings, this moisture can accumulate rapidly if ventilation is insufficient.

Without proper airflow or exhaust systems, humidity can rise throughout a home or commercial space, particularly in bathrooms, kitchens, and laundry areas. These are common hotspots for mould growth due to their moisture-generating activities. Understanding how occupant behaviour affects humidity levels is vital for managing indoor air quality.

HVAC Systems: Double-Edged Swords for Moisture and Mould Control

Heating, ventilation, and air conditioning (HVAC) systems have a complex relationship with indoor moisture and mould. On the one hand, properly designed and maintained HVAC systems help regulate temperature, control humidity, and filter airborne contaminants. On the other hand, poorly maintained or improperly installed systems can exacerbate moisture problems and contribute to mould spread.

Issues such as clogged or dirty filters, blocked drain pans, and leaky ductwork can cause condensation and microbial growth within HVAC components. For example, evaporator coils that become excessively cold can accumulate condensation, providing a breeding ground for mould. This contamination can then be dispersed throughout the building, negatively impacting air quality.

Strategies for Controlling Humidity Through HVAC Maintenance and Design

Regular HVAC maintenance is crucial to controlling moisture levels indoors. Key strategies include:

Routine filter replacement: Dirty filters reduce airflow, cause system strain, and can become reservoirs for dust and microbes.
Cleaning coils and drain pans: Keeping these components clean prevents water accumulation and microbial growth.
Ensuring proper drainage: Drain pans and condensate lines must be clear and functional to remove moisture efficiently.
Balancing ventilation: Properly designed ventilation introduces fresh air and exhausts moist indoor air, preventing humidity buildup.
Incorporating dehumidification: In humid climates or buildings prone to moisture problems, integrating dehumidifiers with HVAC systems helps maintain RH within safe levels.
The Role of HVAC Filters and Air Quality

HVAC filters play a significant role in trapping airborne particles, including mould spores. High-efficiency particulate air (HEPA) filters or filters with a Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value (MERV) rating appropriate for the building can reduce the concentration of biological contaminants in circulated air.

Selecting and maintaining appropriate filtration media ensures that spores and other allergens are captured before air is distributed, improving overall indoor air quality. However, filters must be replaced regularly to maintain effectiveness and avoid becoming a source of contamination themselves.

lessons.title: Health Effects of Mould Exposure — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Understanding the Risks

Mould affects indoor air quality in ways that have direct and sometimes severe consequences for human health. These health effects range from minor irritation to chronic illness, depending on an individual’s sensitivity and the level of exposure. Mould spores, once airborne, can be inhaled deep into the lungs or come into contact with eyes and skin.

Short-Term and Long-Term Symptoms

Short-term effects can include symptoms such as sneezing, coughing, itchy or watery eyes, skin rashes, and nasal congestion. These are often mistaken for seasonal allergies, but if symptoms improve when the person leaves the environment, mould may be the underlying cause.

More prolonged or repeated exposure can lead to chronic respiratory conditions, including asthma and bronchitis. In severe cases—particularly for those with weakened immune systems—exposure to mould can result in fungal infections that affect the lungs or other organs.

High-Risk Populations

Some groups are more susceptible to the effects of mould exposure:

Infants and young children
Elderly individuals
People with asthma or respiratory illnesses
Immunocompromised individuals (e.g., cancer patients or transplant recipients)
For these populations, maintaining excellent indoor air quality is not just a matter of comfort but a critical aspect of health protection.

lessons.title: Inspection Protocols and Air Sampling — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Conducting a Thorough Visual Inspection

Effective mould remediation begins with a comprehensive inspection. This step helps identify both visible signs of mould and potential sources of moisture that may not be immediately apparent. Technicians should look for:

Staining or discolouration on ceilings, walls, and floors
Warped or swollen materials
Peeling paint or wallpaper
Persistent musty odours
Particular attention should be paid to areas with limited ventilation, such as closets, behind furniture, and within HVAC components.

Air Sampling Techniques and Interpretation

Air sampling is used to determine whether mould is present in the air at elevated levels. This process involves drawing air through specialized cassettes that capture spores for lab analysis. The results are compared against outdoor air samples to determine if mould levels inside the building are abnormally high.

It’s important to understand that not all mould problems require air sampling. In many cases, visible mould is sufficient to warrant remediation. However, sampling is especially helpful in real estate transactions, legal cases, or when there’s suspicion of hidden mould.

Results must be interpreted by a qualified professional, as high spore counts do not always correlate with health complaints, and low counts don’t guarantee a clean environment. The type, amount, and pattern of spores are all key to drawing conclusions.

modules.title: Drying Strategies to Prevent Mould Regrowth

lessons.title: Drying Strategies to Prevent Mould Regrowth — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Drying as a First Line of Defense

Drying is the most important and time-sensitive component of any water damage response. The quicker moisture is removed from materials and the air, the lower the chance for mould to grow. Restoration professionals must understand the science behind drying to carry it out effectively.

Effective drying involves reducing the moisture content of materials to acceptable levels. This typically includes the use of air movers to encourage evaporation, dehumidifiers to remove water from the air, and heat to increase the rate of evaporation. The balance of these elements is key.

Monitoring Progress and Verifying Results

Throughout the drying process, technicians must monitor:

Moisture levels in affected materials
Temperature and relative humidity
Equipment performance
Tools such as non-invasive moisture meters, thermal hygrometers, and infrared cameras help track drying progress. Documentation of these readings is crucial for verifying that materials are dry and the environment is stable.

Drying must be continued until moisture levels reach acceptable industry standards. Removing equipment too early can allow materials to reabsorb moisture or allow dormant mould spores to reactivate.

modules.title: Remediation Approaches and Air Quality Recovery

lessons.title: Remediation Approaches and Air Quality Recovery — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Removing Affected Materials Safely

When materials are extensively contaminated or cannot be dried effectively, removal is often the only option. This includes drywall, carpeting, insulation, and ceiling tiles. These items must be carefully bagged and disposed of to prevent the spread of spores.

Proper containment using plastic sheeting, zip walls, and negative air machines is essential during removal. These measures protect clean areas from cross-contamination and maintain safe air quality throughout the process.

Cleaning and Air Filtration

After removal, all surfaces in the affected area must be thoroughly cleaned using HEPA vacuuming, damp wiping with antimicrobial solutions, and air scrubbing. AFDs (air filtration devices) are used during and after remediation to capture airborne particles.

Technicians may also use encapsulants—special coatings designed to seal surfaces and prevent residual spores from becoming airborne. However, encapsulants should only be applied after thorough cleaning and drying have been verified.

Final Clearance and Air Quality Verification

Once remediation is complete, clearance testing is often performed to verify that spore counts have returned to normal. This may involve:

Post-remediation air sampling
Surface sampling
Visual inspection for residual mould
This final step confirms that the air is clean, the moisture problem is resolved, and the space is safe for reoccupation.

lessons.title: Communication and Documentation — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
The Importance of Clear Communication

Clients may not always understand the dangers of mould or the need for extensive remediation. It’s essential to explain the reasons for your actions in terms they can relate to—emphasizing health, property value, and peace of mind.

Visual aids such as moisture maps, infrared images, and air sampling reports can help convey the severity of the issue. Answering questions clearly and confidently helps build trust.

Professional Documentation

A well-documented project is essential for insurance claims, legal protection, and client satisfaction. Good documentation should include:

Before and after photos
Moisture readings and drying logs
Air sampling reports (if conducted)
Equipment usage logs and placement maps
Work authorization and signed remediation plans
Proper documentation is more than just paperwork—it reflects the professionalism and thoroughness of your work.

Educating for Prevention

Finally, take time to educate clients on how to prevent future mould growth. This includes tips on ventilation, dehumidifier use, regular maintenance, and prompt response to water intrusion.

By closing the project with clear advice and documentation, you position yourself as a trusted expert and improve long-term outcomes for the client.

courses.title: Using Air Scrubbers and AFDs to Improve Job Site Air Quality
courses.description:
Welcome to Using Air Scrubbers and AFDs to Improve Job Site Air Quality — a practical, in-depth course designed to equip restoration professionals, contractors, and environmental technicians with the knowledge and skills to effectively manage airborne contaminants on job sites.

Indoor air quality is a critical factor in restoration, remediation, and construction projects. Whether you’re addressing mould contamination, fire and smoke damage, asbestos, or general dust and debris, controlling the air environment is essential for health, safety, and compliance.

Air Filtration Devices (AFDs), commonly known as air scrubbers, are the front line in protecting workers and building occupants from airborne hazards. This course demystifies how they work, when and where to use them, and how to get the most out of these essential tools.

In this course, you’ll learn how to:

Understand the components, mechanics, and modes of operation of air scrubbers

Choose and manage filters (HEPA, carbon, pre-filters) for different job scenarios

Set up units effectively on site for both recirculation and negative pressure

Calculate Air Changes per Hour (ACH) to meet air quality standards

Maintain equipment and manage filters for long-term performance

Ensure safety, compliance, and proper documentation throughout every project

Communicate the value of clean air strategies to clients and stakeholders

This is more than just an equipment overview — it’s a course that builds confidence and competency in using AFDs to improve job performance, protect health, and meet regulatory demands.

By the end of the training, you’ll not only understand how to operate air scrubbers effectively — you’ll be able to justify their use with measurable results and communicate your process clearly to clients and insurers alike.

Let’s get started and breathe cleaner, safer air into every job site.

modules.title: Introduction to Air Filtration Devices (AFDs) and Air Scrubbers

lessons.title: Introduction to Air Filtration Devices (AFDs) and Air Scrubbers — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Understanding AFDs and Air Scrubbers

Air Filtration Devices (AFDs), commonly referred to as air scrubbers, are essential tools in the cleaning, restoration, and construction industries. These devices are designed to remove contaminants from the air, making the indoor environment safer for both workers and occupants. They function by drawing in air, filtering it through various filtration stages, and then releasing clean air either back into the space or outside the structure.

Air scrubbers are widely used during remediation efforts involving mould, asbestos, lead dust, or general construction dust. Their importance has grown as awareness of airborne health hazards has increased. Beyond particle removal, they also help reduce odors and chemical vapors when equipped with the right filters.

The Role of AFDs in Job Site Safety

Maintaining clean air on job sites is not only a matter of comfort but of health and regulatory compliance. Contaminants such as fine dust, mould spores, or off-gassed chemicals can pose serious respiratory risks. Prolonged exposure can lead to health complications for workers, and may also result in legal or financial repercussions for companies if safety standards are not met. Deploying AFDs demonstrates a proactive approach to occupational health and can enhance worker productivity by providing a cleaner, safer environment.

When and Where AFDs Are Needed

AFDs are necessary in any environment where airborne contaminants may be present. These include post-flood environments where mould can flourish, fire restoration projects where smoke particles linger, and during renovations or demolitions where building materials are disturbed. AFDs also play a critical role in sensitive environments such as healthcare settings or clean rooms, where even low levels of airborne contaminants can pose serious risks.

lessons.title: Components and Mechanics of AFDs — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
The Inner Workings of an Air Scrubber

An air scrubber is made up of several integral parts that work together to purify air. The process begins at the air intake, where contaminated air is drawn into the machine. As the air moves through the unit, it encounters a series of filters. The first filter, often called the pre-filter, is designed to catch larger particles like dust and hair. This protects the more sensitive filters downstream and prolongs their lifespan.

Next is the HEPA filter, which is the core filtration component. High-Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA) filters are capable of capturing 99.97% of airborne particles that are 0.3 microns or larger. This includes many harmful contaminants such as mould spores, bacteria, and fine dust. Some units may also include an activated carbon filter to address odours and volatile organic compounds (VOCs).

Air Movement and Design

The blower or fan is another crucial component, responsible for moving air through the device and ensuring consistent airflow. The housing unit, usually made from durable plastic or metal, keeps all internal parts secure and ensures safe operation. Understanding how these parts function together helps technicians operate and troubleshoot air scrubbers effectively on the job.

Built-in Safety and Efficiency Features

Modern AFDs often include features that enhance usability and safety. These might include variable speed settings, filter change indicators, and built-in hour meters. These features not only help in maintaining efficiency but also ensure that the devices are being used correctly. Units may also include protective grilles to prevent contact with the fan or internal components, and ergonomic handles or wheels for easier transport.

modules.title: Types of Filters and Their Functions

lessons.title: Types of Filters and Their Functions — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Multi-Stage Filtration Strategy

Effective air scrubbing depends on the use of multiple filtration stages, each with a distinct purpose. The first layer, the pre-filter, plays a foundational role by capturing larger debris and reducing the particle load that reaches the finer filters. Pre-filters are usually inexpensive and require frequent replacement, sometimes daily, depending on job site conditions.

The HEPA filter, located after the pre-filter, is responsible for capturing the most dangerous fine particles. Its tight mesh construction traps microscopic contaminants that are invisible to the naked eye. These filters are indispensable on job sites involving mould remediation, fire restoration, or lead dust.

Additional Filter Options

Some air scrubbers are equipped with additional filters designed for specific tasks. Activated carbon filters absorb VOCs and neutralize strong odours, making them ideal for chemical spills or smoke-damaged buildings. In more advanced models, there may be optional UV light features for microbial deactivation, or electrostatic filters to help trap charged particles. Understanding the options available allows technicians to select the right filter combination for each specific task.

Filter Performance and Ratings

It’s important to understand how filters are rated for performance. HEPA filters are standardized to meet specific efficiency thresholds, but other filters may vary in quality and durability. Understanding MERV ratings (Minimum Efficiency Reporting Value) can help in evaluating non-HEPA filters. Higher MERV ratings indicate a filter’s ability to capture smaller particles, although not all MERV-rated filters are suitable for use in AFDs. Always consult the manufacturer’s specifications.

modules.title: Modes of Operation: Recirculation vs Negative Pressure

lessons.title: Modes of Operation: Recirculation vs Negative Pressure — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Recirculation Mode Explained

In recirculation mode, an AFD pulls air from the room, filters it, and then discharges the clean air back into the same space. This mode is particularly useful when you need to continuously purify the air in an enclosed environment, such as during sanding, demolition, or cleaning activities. Recirculation helps reduce overall particulate concentrations but does not prevent contaminants from moving to adjacent areas.

This mode is especially helpful during ongoing work where dust and fine debris are continuously being generated. Recirculating the air through the scrubber allows for continuous removal of airborne particles and contributes to a more comfortable working environment. However, it is important to note that recirculation does not control the direction of airflow in a room, and should not be relied on for containment.

Establishing Negative Pressure

Negative pressure mode is used when the goal is containment. By venting filtered air to the outside, the AFD creates a pressure differential between the workspace and surrounding areas. This causes air to flow into the work area rather than allowing contaminated air to escape. Negative pressure is essential during mould remediation, asbestos abatement, or infectious disease control.

Creating an effective negative pressure environment requires more than just attaching a duct to the exhaust. Seals around doorways, windows, and barriers must be airtight to prevent leaks. The airflow rate must be sufficient to maintain the pressure difference, and continuous monitoring is needed to confirm that the system is working correctly.

Making the Right Choice for Your Job

Choosing between these modes depends on the specific risks present and the need for containment. In many cases, jobs begin with recirculation and transition to negative pressure once containment barriers are in place. Understanding when and how to use each mode is a key competency for any technician working in air quality or restoration.

modules.title: Proper Setup and Placement on the Job Site

lessons.title: Proper Setup and Placement on the Job Site — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Strategic Positioning of Units

Effective use of AFDs requires thoughtful placement. Air scrubbers should be positioned to maximise airflow across the contaminated area. Avoid placing units directly against walls or in corners where airflow is restricted. Ideally, the intake side should face the area of highest contamination, while the clean air exhaust should be directed away from workers.

Understanding the layout of the space and the workflow of the project is essential. Airflow should be managed in a way that contaminants are drawn away from occupied zones or toward containment barriers. Sometimes multiple AFDs are needed in larger spaces or complex layouts. The goal is to create a clean air pathway that continuously moves contaminated air toward the scrubber and away from sensitive areas.

Use of Ducting for Ventilation

When negative pressure is required, ducting becomes essential. Ducts allow filtered air to be safely vented outside the containment zone. Proper ducting setup involves securely attaching the duct to the exhaust port and sealing all joints to prevent leaks. The length and diameter of the ducting can impact airflow, so choosing the right size is important.

Sharp bends or excessive lengths can reduce efficiency and create back pressure, which affects the performance of the AFD. Ducting should be inspected regularly to ensure it remains clear and properly connected. In some cases, it may be necessary to route ducting through windows, access hatches, or temporary barriers.

Safety and Practical Considerations

On active job sites, it’s crucial to prevent the air scrubber from becoming a hazard itself. Place units on stable, level surfaces and use caution signage to keep pathways clear. Ensure that electrical cords do not create tripping hazards, and avoid areas where water may pose a shock risk.

In high-traffic environments, consider using barricades or placing the AFD behind existing structures to avoid accidental damage. Protecting the unit also ensures continued operation and prevents delays that can arise from equipment failure.

lessons.title: Calculating Air Changes per Hour (ACH) — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
What is ACH and Why Does It Matter?

Air Changes per Hour (ACH) is a metric used to determine how many times the air in a given space is completely replaced with filtered air within one hour. It’s a foundational concept in indoor air quality management, especially when using AFDs or air scrubbers in restoration and remediation work.

Higher ACH rates mean that airborne contaminants are being removed more frequently, which reduces exposure for occupants and speeds up cleaning and remediation. Understanding and calculating ACH ensures you’re using your air scrubbers effectively.

How to Calculate ACH

To determine ACH, you need two pieces of information: the airflow rate (in cubic feet per minute or CFM) of your air scrubber, and the volume of the room in cubic feet.

Use the formula: ACH = (CFM × 60) / Room Volume (in cubic feet)

For example, if your scrubber delivers 500 CFM and your room is 10,000 cubic feet: (500 × 60) / 10,000 = 3 ACH

This tells you the air in the room is being fully replaced three times per hour.

Target ACH Rates by Project Type

Different types of jobs require different ACH goals:

General construction or cleaning: 2–4 ACH
Mould remediation: 4–6 ACH
Asbestos abatement or infection control: 6–12 ACH
Real-World Application

Accurately calculating ACH helps justify equipment use, ensures compliance with industry guidelines (e.g., IICRC S520), and provides clients with measurable results. It also helps identify if additional units are needed to meet air quality targets

lessons.title: Maintenance and Filter Management — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Importance of Routine Maintenance

Maintaining your AFDs is crucial to ensure they perform at their highest capacity throughout the duration of each job. Regular maintenance helps prevent filter overload, protects internal components, and reduces the risk of recirculating contaminated air. A poorly maintained air scrubber can become ineffective—or worse, it can become a source of contamination itself.

Proper maintenance not only protects workers and clients, but also reduces operating costs over time. Early detection of wear, tear, or clogging allows for timely intervention and extends the unit’s life span.

Filter Management: Lifespan and Replacement Guidelines

AFDs generally utilize a multi-stage filtration system:

Pre-filters: Designed to trap large debris and prolong the life of the HEPA filter. These filters are relatively inexpensive and often need to be replaced daily on dusty sites.
HEPA filters: These high-efficiency filters require careful monitoring. While they can last multiple jobs, they should be replaced when airflow noticeably drops, contamination persists, or manufacturer timelines recommend it.
Carbon filters (if used): These help reduce odours and VOCs. Their lifespan depends on the type and intensity of contaminants being captured.
Signs a filter may need replacing include:

Visible soiling or discoloration
Reduced suction or airflow
Changes in pressure readings
Persistent odours or air quality complaints
Cleaning Procedures and Storage Protocol

To prevent cross-contamination between job sites, units must be thoroughly cleaned after each use. Turn off and unplug the unit. Remove filters (with PPE) and seal them in plastic for proper disposal. Disinfect the exterior and interior components using a suitable antimicrobial solution. Pay close attention to fan blades, housing surfaces, and corners where dust tends to accumulate.

After cleaning, allow the unit to dry completely before storage. Store AFDs in a dry, temperature-controlled area to prevent moisture buildup or microbial growth. Units should be stacked or shelved carefully to avoid structural damage.

Record-Keeping and Scheduling

Keeping a detailed log of filter changes, maintenance activities, and unit usage is a best practice. This helps with warranty tracking, ensures accountability among technicians, and assists in providing documentation for clients or regulators.

Sample maintenance log entries may include:

Date and time of filter change
Job site location
Name of technician performing maintenance
Observed issues or repairs completed
Proper maintenance habits directly impact performance and compliance, and they reflect the professionalism of your team.

lessons.title: Safety Considerations and Compliance — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
AFDs and Worker Safety

Air scrubbers contribute to a safer worksite, but they must be integrated into a broader health and safety strategy. Technicians must not assume that the presence of an AFD eliminates all airborne hazards. Appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE)—including N95 or P100 respirators, gloves, goggles, and disposable coveralls—must still be worn, especially during demolition or cleanup involving hazardous materials.

Understanding the limits of an AFD is vital. While effective at particle removal, AFDs do not eliminate all risks, such as direct contact with mould or chemical residues. They should be viewed as part of a layered protection plan.

Electrical Safety Precautions

Given that AFDs are powered devices often used in high-moisture environments, electrical safety is critical. Units should be connected only to grounded outlets. Extension cords, if used, must be heavy-duty and rated for the amperage draw of the equipment.

Key safety measures include:

Inspecting cords before each use for frays, exposed wires, or loose plugs
Never operating units near standing water
Avoiding daisy-chaining multiple devices on a single outlet or power board
During long jobs, cables should be secured to prevent tripping hazards. Using cord covers or safety cones is recommended on high-traffic job sites.

Understanding and Meeting Compliance Requirements

Different restoration scenarios are subject to distinct regulatory and procedural standards. Technicians must be familiar with the governing bodies and standards that apply to their specific project type.

Examples include:

IICRC S520: Standards for mould remediation projects
EPA/OSHA guidelines: For asbestos abatement and lead removal
State or local health regulations: For healthcare facility construction or hazardous material handling
Failure to comply can result in fines, shutdowns, or legal action. More importantly, it places the health of workers and building occupants at risk.

Documentation and Liability Protection

Documentation plays a vital role in demonstrating regulatory compliance and best practices. Maintain detailed records of:

Equipment used, including model and serial numbers
Filter installation and replacement dates
Negative pressure monitoring logs
Safety inspections or incident reports
These records are often required in insurance claims or third-party audits and provide crucial legal protection for your company.

Maintaining a culture of safety around AFDs not only protects lives but also strengthens your brand’s reputation as a responsible and knowledgeable service provider.

lessons.title: Monitoring Effectiveness on Site — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Verifying Performance with Instruments

The effectiveness of air scrubbers should be measured using tools, not assumptions. Particle counters can detect concentrations of PM10 and PM2.5 in real time, showing a clear before-and-after comparison.

Manometers or pressure gauges verify that negative pressure has been achieved in a containment setup. Aim for a pressure differential of -0.02 inches of water column or greater.

Visual and Practical Observations

Technicians should also look for visual cues:

Reduced visible dust and haze in the air
Reduced musty or chemical odours
Quieter fan noise indicating reduced airflow (potential sign of clogged filters)
Adjustments and Documentation

If the unit’s impact is lower than expected:

Check for obstructions at the intake or exhaust
Replace pre-filters
Ensure containment seals are intact
All findings should be documented in the job report, including:

Initial air quality levels
Time of activation
ACH calculations
Final readings or conditions

modules.title: Communicating with Clients and Documenting Your Work

lessons.title: Communicating with Clients and Documenting Your Work — Reading
lessons.contentBody:
Explaining the Role of AFDs

Clients may not immediately understand the purpose of a large, noisy machine in their space. Clear communication is key. Explain that the AFD:

Removes harmful particles and improves air quality
Helps speed up remediation or drying
Contributes to safety and health compliance
Providing Transparent Reports

A thorough air scrubber log should include:

Date of deployment
Filter types used and change dates
ACH target and actual readings
Photos of setup (if needed for insurance or documentation)
These records demonstrate professionalism, support billing, and provide a layer of protection in the event of disputes.

Building Trust and Long-Term Relationships

Taking the time to show clients the equipment, walk them through air quality readings, and explain your reasoning for placement or pressure targets builds credibility. Clients are more likely to recommend and rehire contractors who offer transparency, data-driven insights, and visible efforts to protect their health.
